issue_title: Replace Image that has a Thumbnail
issue_body:
I'm using the thumbnail filter to generate thumbnails for images and I've noticed that when i replace an existing image, the thumbnail file-id changes, even though the image file-id stays the same.

I had a look at the thumbnail filter and it seems to me that it would be easy to modify it so that it would replace the thumbnail instead of creating a new one. If not for the builtin filter, I would at least like to do that for my own, to be able to permanently redirect to thumbnails.

The only problem is that there's currently no way to access the old file in a filter. However, the value is still present higher up in the stack.

How would you feel about passing down that value to `_apply_filters` [from the `_field_set` method in sqlalchemy.py](https://github.com/amol-/depot/blob/6f3587e/depot/fields/sqlalchemy.py#L75)?

It wouldn't be backwards compatible with existing filters unless we inspect their signature, but apart from that I see no drawbacks. If you think this would make a good addition I'm happy to provide a PR.

issue_title: DNS resolution not working
issue_body:
When I checkout using this library then this code in common.py returns an error:
```
p = subprocess.Popen(cmd,
                             stdout=subprocess.PIPE,
                             stderr=subprocess.STDOUT,
                             env={'LANG': 'en_US.UTF-8'})

 stdout = p.stdout.read()
     r = p.wait()
      if r != success_code:
         raise SvnException("Command failed with (%s): %s\n%s".format(
              (p.returncode, cmd, stdout)))
```

stdout contains:
`svn: E731001: Unable to connect to a repository at URL 'svn://dummydomain.corp/repos/testing/robot-
 framework-test/trunk'
svn: E731001: Unknown hostname 'dummydomain.corp'`

Invoking the very same command specified by 'cmd' manually in a shell works.

I think the problem is that the Popen call is using the parameter env and therefore effectively completely replacing the environment with the single variable 'LANG'.
I could fix the problem by just not using the env parameter:
`p = subprocess.Popen(cmd,
                             stdout=subprocess.PIPE,
                             stderr=subprocess.STDOUT)`

As a sidenote:
This does not produce a valid error string:
`raise SvnException("Command failed with (%s): %s\n%s".format(
                (p.returncode, cmd, stdout)))`
I think you cannot mix %s-syntax with format.

issue_title: SearchEndpoint kwargs bug
issue_body:
I found a few bugs regarding the SearchEndpoint parsing keyword arguments.

When I want to execute such a query:

`zenpy_client.search(
                type="ticket",
                updated_greater_than=last_fetch_time
                sort_by="id",
                sort_order="asc"
            )
`

where last_fetch_time is a datetime field
We are stuck in the elif clause:

`elif isinstance(value, datetime):
`

which is kind of wrong, because the if clause there is actually about keys and not the values. A similar problem arises in the 

`elif isinstance(value, list):
`

and

`elif isinstance(value, list) and key == 'ids':
`

the second clause is probably never executed because it enters the first if clause.

A workaround to bypass this datetime problem is to call the search method this way:

`zenpy_client.search(
                type="ticket",
                updated_greater_than=last_fetch_time.strftime("%Y-%m-%d")
                sort_by="id",
                sort_order="asc"
            )
`

which will work as expected as we are bypassing the datetime check.

leaf subinterface

  path /oc-if:interfaces/oc-if:interface[oc-if:name=current()/../interface]/oc-if:subinterfaces/oc-if:subinterface/oc-if:index

issue_title: ldap3.add modifies the passed in values
issue_body:
When you have code like this:

```python
conn = ldap3.Connection(....)
initial = {'attr': [value, value], 'other_attr': [value, value]}
print(initial)
conn.add('dn', [list, of, object, Classes], initial)
print(initial)
```

The expectation would be that the two `print` statements result in identical output. However, when passing my dict of attributes into `add` that dict gets modified by the add operation to include the object classes I passed in as a list (the second argument).

This showed up in some of my test cases where I compiled an initial dict of attributes, did the add and after the add had it log that an entity was created with `initial.keys()`. At that point my tests started to fail b/c  `sorted(initial.keys())` before and after the add operation were no longer the same.

Imho this shouldn't happen. If `ldap3.add` goes about and changes the dict I push into it, it should use `copy.deepcopy()` to create its own since there's no way for ldap3 to know if I might be using that dict somewhere else too, or access it later. I've fixed this in my own code by doing an `add(... copy.deepcopy(inital))` as a workaround.

I'm using pretty much identical code to the example `face_detection_ex.cpp`, with the only difference that I'm constructing the `dlib::array2d<dlib::rgb_pixel>` myself, instead of reading it from a file using `dlib::image_load`. The example program compiles fine and works, but in the application I'm trying to embed this, it isn't working: it returns an empty vector of rectangles.

Note: I stripped the triangle_up call (from both my code and the example code) as my input images are large enough.

Now, what really blows my mind is: I'm outputting the image2d I'm constructing myself, to a file as a verification. The file looks perfectly fine, but still dlib doesn't find any faces. However, when running the example program to this very same file, it perfectly detects the faces.

I am using in my CMakeLists.txt (note: the .git folder for dlib is at: `ext/dlib/.git`)

```
ADD_SUBDIRECTORY("ext/dlib/dlib")
INCLUDE_DIRECTORIES("ext/dlib/")

TARGET_LINK_LIBRARIES(SilverNode
    // ...
    dlib::dlib)
```

All this compiles fine, links fine, and runs fine, except it doesn't detect faces. The only difference I am aware of is that I'm using `ADD_SUBDIRECTORY` instead of `INCLUDE` as proposed by the example cmake file. When I use `INCLUDE` this gives some build error because my project uses C++14, and dlib sets the compiler settings to C++11 globally (and thus also for my project, which causes the error).

issue_title: Check type and value at the same time
issue_body:
Right now, when checking types with `is_a()`, the return value is `None` for a scalar value, and an `IterableInspector` for an iterable value. In neither case can I then go on to check the value of the subject.

For a scalar:

```
In [9]: ensure(9).is_an(int).which.equals(9)
---------------------------------------------------------------------------
AttributeError                            Traceback (most recent call last)
<ipython-input-9-c205f4e9cc21> in <module>()
----> 1 ensure(9).is_an(int).which.equals(9)

AttributeError: 'NoneType' object has no attribute 'which'
```


For an iterable:

```
In [7]: ensure('foo').is_a(str).which.equals('foo')
---------------------------------------------------------------------------
AttributeError                            Traceback (most recent call last)
<ipython-input-7-48f991a6e079> in <module>()
----> 1 ensure('foo').is_a(str).which.equals('foo')

AttributeError: 'IterableInspector' object has no attribute 'which'
```

The examples are trivial, but I find myself wanting to check the type and value frequently as a part of a chain. I'm used to being able to do this with a fluent assertion interface like [Chai](http://chaijs.com/api/bdd/), and I'd love to see ensure reach that level of fluency.

A good start would be to never return None, returning the wrapped subject instead, and to add `which` and `whose_value` properties on all the inspectors which take you back to the wrapped subject again.

issue_title: Nested templates yield token exception
issue_body:
From C++11 on, nested templates do not require a whitespace between closing '>' anymore.

Pre C++11:
```C++
std::vector<std::vector<double> >
```
C++11:
```C++
std::vector<std::vector<double>>
```
The second version yields a token exception.

issue_title: Error on gpx 1.0 format parsing because of <course> float value
issue_body:
Hi,

Thanks a lot for gpxpy that i use a lot.

I noticed there was a possible mistake in the parsing about the value of "course" tags. The GPX 1.0 format (http://www.topografix.com/gpx_manual.asp) tells us its unit is degrees, so in other words, a float. But the gpxpy parser apparently tries to read it as an int.

```
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "<stdin>", line 1, in <module>
  File "/usr/local/lib/python2.7/dist-packages/gpxpy/__init__.py", line 34, in parse
    return parser.parse()
  File "/usr/local/lib/python2.7/dist-packages/gpxpy/parser.py", line 211, in parse
    raise mod_gpx.GPXXMLSyntaxException('Error parsing XML: %s' % str(e), e)
gpxpy.gpx.GPXXMLSyntaxException: Error parsing XML: Invalid value for <course>... 67.72383 (invalid literal for int() with base 10: '67.72383')
```

Apparently the "course" tag is not included anymore in GPX 1.1 (last implementation) but there are still lots of programs producing GPX 1.0 files.

Do you agree with me that "course" values should be red as floats in gpxpy ?

issue_title: Doctype test fails after using vcrpy cassette
issue_body:
I made a simple function that returns a page doctype. A test for that function works fine without vcrpy. It fails with vcrpy because a different doctype is returned.

This is the function inside `amzn.py` file:

    import requests
    from lxml import etree, html

    def get_doctype():
        r = requests.get('http://www.amazon.com')
        html_element = html.fromstring(r.text)
        tree = etree.ElementTree(html_element)
        doctype = tree.docinfo.doctype
        return doctype

And this is the test for that function. It's inside `test_amzn.py` file:

    from amzn import get_doctype

    def test_amazon_doctype():
        assert get_doctype() == 'random string'

Predictably, it fails:

    ============================= test session starts ==============================
    platform linux -- Python 3.4.3 -- py-1.4.26 -- pytest-2.7.0
    rootdir: /home/as/Projects/scrapping, inifile: 
    collected 1 items
    
    Projects/scrapping/test_amzn.py F

    =================================== FAILURES ===================================
    _____________________________ test_amazon_doctype ______________________________

        def test_amazon_doctype():
    >       assert get_doctype() == 'random string'
    E       assert '<!DOCTYPE html>' == 'random string'
    E         - <!DOCTYPE html>
    E         + random string

    Projects/scrapping/test_amzn.py:7: AssertionError
    =========================== 1 failed in 1.73 seconds ===========================

So let's fix it:

    def test_amazon_doctype():
        assert get_doctype() == '<!DOCTYPE html>'

Now it passes successfully. The test is written correctly:

    ============================= test session starts ==============================
    platform linux -- Python 3.4.3 -- py-1.4.26 -- pytest-2.7.0
    rootdir: /home/as/Projects/scrapping, inifile: 
    collected 1 items

    Projects/scrapping/test_amzn.py .

    =========================== 1 passed in 1.60 seconds ===========================

Now let's apply vcrpy's cassette:

    from amzn import get_doctype
    import vcr

    @vcr.use_cassette('doctype.yaml')
    def test_amazon_doctype():
        assert get_doctype() == '<!DOCTYPE html>'

The test fails - a different doctype is returned:

    ============================= test session starts ==============================
    platform linux -- Python 3.4.3 -- py-1.4.26 -- pytest-2.7.0
    rootdir: /home/as/Projects/scrapping, inifile: 
    collected 1 items

    Projects/scrapping/test_amzn.py F

    =================================== FAILURES ===================================
    _____________________________ test_amazon_doctype ______________________________

        @vcr.use_cassette('doctype.yaml')
        def test_amazon_doctype():
    >       assert get_doctype() == '<!DOCTYPE html>'
    E       assert '<!DOCTYPE ht...0/loose.dtd">' == '<!DOCTYPE html>'
    E         - <!DOCTYPE html PUBLIC "-//W3C//DTD HTML 4.0 Transitional//EN" "http://www.w3.org/TR/REC-html40/loose.dtd">
    E         + <!DOCTYPE html>

    Projects/scrapping/test_amzn.py:7: AssertionError
    =========================== 1 failed in 0.52 seconds ===========================

I've checked the body of the response inside .yaml file. Here's the beginning of the body after decoding and unpacking it:

    b'\n\n\n\n  \n  \n\n\n  \n  \n\n\n\n      <!doctype html><!--[if lt IE 7]><html class="a-no-js a-lt-ie10 a-lt-ie9 a-lt-ie8 a-lt-ie7

That's definitely a different doctype from the one returned after applying vcrpy's cassette.

Of course, if I modify the test:

    def test_amazon_doctype():
        assert get_doctype() == '<!DOCTYPE html PUBLIC "-//W3C//DTD HTML 4.0 Transitional//EN" "http://www.w3.org/TR/REC-html40/loose.dtd">'

and run it without vcrpy, it fails:

    ============================= test session starts ==============================
    platform linux -- Python 3.4.3 -- py-1.4.26 -- pytest-2.7.0
    rootdir: /home/as/Projects/scrapping, inifile: 
    collected 1 items

    >       assert get_doctype() == '<!DOCTYPE html PUBLIC "-//W3C//DTD HTML 4.0 Transitional//EN" "http://www.w3.org/TR/REC-html40/loose.dtd">'

issue_title: Downbeats -- representation and evaluation
issue_body:
Firstly, this issue straddles `mir_eval` and `jams`, (+ @craffel @bmcfee ), but I think it fits better here.

Downbeat estimation --marking beats that begin bars / measures-- can be seen as a form of beat tracking. In the wild, there are a few ways in which downbeats have been encoded in plaintext formats: (a) single column of timestamps, in seconds; (b) double columns of timestamps and beat number (e.g. 1-4); or (c), double columns of timestamp and `{measure}.{beat}` values. Alternatively, JAMS has a two different ways to represent this, either through the `beat` or `position` namespace; notably, in the former, specifying a value of `1` indicates that the observed beat is a downbeat, and `NaN`s (or any other number) are used to represent arbitrary beats.

Having identified two ordered lists of downbeats, the evaluation would cleanly follow beats. However, it's a bit unclear to me where the adapter layer should live in taking a beat annotation (which potentially represents anonymous beats, downbeats, or a mix of both) and slicing out only the relevant observations. 

Two options I see:

1. Add a `downbeat.py` module to mir_eval, which handles representation wrangling, and wraps the evaluation machinery in `beat.py`.
2. Push this data wrangling off as the responsibility of the representation format, e.g. JAMS. Beat and downbeat annotations funnel through `mir_eval.beat`, and `mir_eval` doesn't really care which are passed in.

A third point worth mentioning falls somewhere between the two: 
3. Revisit beat vs downbeat representations. Separate annotations seem like redundant information (all downbeats should be beats), but overloading a beat annotation isn't necessarily easy to work with.

Having sketched this out, the second option (2) is attractive from an `mir_eval` standpoint. However it distributes the responsibility over the different formats, which means it's unlikely to reach one good conclusion.

Thoughts? Has anyone already solved this problem and I've missed it in my travels?

issue_title: server: graceful reload and restart via SIGHUP/SIGUSR1 signals
issue_body:
* Graceful reload: when the main program receives SIGUSR1, let it shutdown all workers and extra processes, call a restart callback, and re-spawn all workers and extra processes again. A restart callback is intended to read the (changed) configuration again, which is written by the user.
 * Graceful restart: when the main program receives SIGHUP, let it terminate completely and re-execute the whole process again, preferably with the same `sys.argv` and environment variables. Add an optional restart callback which is called after terminating workers but before calling user-defined main exit handler.

There may be required several design decision on what arguments to pass to callbacks and when to call them. Let's discuss and implement.

issue_title: instructions for installing on Windows
issue_body:
Sharing some wisdom from Thomas Beatty about how to get batman up and running on windows:

The root problem is that Windows does not include a c++ compiler out-of-the-box, so installing via pip and from source will fail when it tries to compile the limb-darkening functions. For Windows, the primary c++ compilers are Microsoft Visual C++ (MSVC) and MinGW (gc++ for Win). If you're using Anaconda, everything since Anaconda 2.4 (python 3.5) has been compiled using MSVC by whomever makes Anaconda for Windows, so it greatly simplifies things to also use MSVC for Batman.

You can install the standalone MSVC compiler from http://landinghub.visualstudio.com/visual-cpp-build-tools. You then edit the Anaconda distutils.cfg (Anaconda3\Lib\distutils\distutils.cfg for me) so that it reads:

[build]
compiler=msvc

At this point you should be able to install from either pip or source.

Incidentally, trying to use MinGW to do the compilation by installing it and setting "compiler=mingw32" in distutils.cfg is a real headache. There are a series of old MSVC dlls (Windows' library files) that MinGW wants to use that are no longer included in either the out-of-the-box Windows, or in the current MSVC distribution. Specially, MinGW wants to use a sequence of "msvcrt..." dlls that have been replaced by a bunch of alternate files (for example vcruntime140.dll, appcrt140.dll, and desktopcrt140.dll). There may be a way around this if someone is a diehard MinGW user, but since using MSVC seems a lot easier, I didn't bother figuring it out.

issue_title: Multi Threading Reading Returns Empty Data
issue_body:
When pooling to get a list of LASFile objects, the threaded objects return empty curve data. The header data is there. Only the numeric curve data is missing.

```python
import glob
from multiprocessing import Pool
import lasio

def get_log_object(path):
    return lasio.read(path)

def main():
    las_files = glob.glob(r'*.las')

    ### read files over loop ###
    print('When Reading In Loop')
    for las_file in las_files[:2]:
        log = get_log_object(las_file)
        print(log.well.uwi.value)
        print(log[0])

    ### pooling multiprocessing ###
    pool = Pool(4)
    logs = pool.map(get_log_object, las_files)
    pool.close()
    pool.join()

    print('When Multiprocessed')
    for log in logs[:2]:
        print(log.well.uwi.value)
        print(log[0])

if __name__ == "__main__":
    main()
```

**Output**
```
When Reading In Loop
35047244830000
[6675.  6675.5 6676.  ... 7287.  7287.5 7288. ]
35047245210000
[5893.  5893.5 5894.  ... 6399.5 6400.  6400.5]
When Multiprocessed
35047244830000
[]
35047245210000
[]
```
Doing 1000+ logs over a single thread loop takes some time. Is there a better way to multithread batch reading of logs? If this is a bug, is there a place to start looking for fixes?

issue_body:
When running the tool the wrong value is being displayed for my IP address.
I noticed where this tool states "Testing from _isp_ (_ip_)…" that the IP shown was not my actual IP address.
I did some investigation into the cause and discovered this appears to be the IP address returned by the `speedtest-config.php` file.
I tested what was happening by running the following commands:

``` sh
curl -s "https://www.speedtest.net/speedtest-config.php" | egrep --color="auto" 'ip=".*?"' #this is the IP returned from speedtest
dig @resolver1.opendns.com myip.opendns.com +short #this is my actual IP
```

The results:

``` text
<client ip="81.xxx.yyy.146" lat="51.4964" lon="-0.1224" isp="PlusNet" isprating="3.2" rating="5" ispdlavg="11049" ispulavg="2413" loggedin="0" />
80.xxx.yyy.198
```

For some reason the connection from the command line causes speedtest.net to return a different IP address.
The strange thing is, if I access that file from any of my web browsers (tested in Safari, and Firefox) the **correct** IP address is displayed.
At first I wondered if this was being caused by the `User-Agent` but if I pass the same one as my browser uses I see no change in the results. I've tried sending all the same request headers that were present in the web browser request but that still resulted in the wrong IP being returned so I've run out of ideas what is causing this difference in behaviour between browser and command line.

issue_title: Can't auto deploy in empty virtualenv
issue_body:
Hi,
When I want to automatic deploy on server django project, which contains 'py-googletrans' library in requirements.txt, or simply run `pip install -r requirements.txt` on new empty virtualenv, I get:
```
$ pip install googletrans --no-cache-dir
Collecting googletrans
  Downloading googletrans-2.1.2.tar.gz
    Complete output from command python setup.py egg_info:
    Traceback (most recent call last):
      File "<string>", line 1, in <module>
      File "/tmp/pip-build-5cKA5j/googletrans/setup.py", line 3, in <module>
        import googletrans
      File "googletrans/__init__.py", line 7, in <module>
        from googletrans.client import Translator
      File "googletrans/client.py", line 7, in <module>
        import requests
    ImportError: No module named requests
```
This error is caused by chain of imports:
 setup.py > __init__.py > client.py > requests
So setup.py run requests before instaling it.

I know that in [this](https://github.com/ssut/py-googletrans#installation) paragraph of docs @ssut remind that if I want to install 'googletrans' I must install requests before, but this is very uncomfortable during automatic deploy.

In my pull request, #23 I add solution on this issue.

issue_title: Handle invalid domain (e.g. ".domain.com")
issue_body:
Around 12:09 UTC, all ElectrumX servers dropped from IRC due to a peer that joined with a hostname in the realname akin to `.domain.com`, and the logs show `ERROR:IRC:encoding with 'idna' codec failed (UnicodeError: label empty or too long)`.

More info on this exception: https://stackoverflow.com/questions/25103126/label-empty-or-too-long-python-urllib2/25103444#25103444

Source of exception: https://github.com/kyuupichan/electrumx/blob/fac3c70c652a72c74e49369c9eecd98f5649ec49/server/irc.py#L157

I propose we catch `UnicodeError` and ignore the peer altogether since the hostname is very likely to be invalid and unconnectable to anyone (and might even crash clients).

While we're at it, maybe we want another `except:` block to account for all exceptions that may be raised by `socket.gethostbyname()`.

issue_title: Proposal: support relative path in 'source' statement
issue_body:
Hi,

I'm working on a project where build configuration system is implemented entirely in Python with Kconfiglib at its center. That way, we don't have to use any of Linux kernel Kconfig/Kbuild code, which has certain benefits. In our situation it's tempting to extend Kconfiglib with new features beyond what's offered by Linux kernel, and one such extension that may be useful for someone else is the support for relative paths in `source` directive.

Imagine the following directory structure:
```
Project
|
+--Kconfig
+--src
   +--Kconfig
   |
   +--SubSystem1
   |  +--Kconfig
   |  |
   |  +--ModuleA
   |  |  +--Kconfig
   |  |
   |  +--ModuleB
   |     +--Kconfig   
   |
   +--SubSystem2
      +--Kconfig
      |
      +--ModuleC
      |  +--Kconfig
      |
      +--ModuleD
         +--Kconfig
```
Here, Kconfig file in `SubSystem1` sources Kconfig file from `ModuleA`, and to do this one writes:
```
source "src/SubSystem1/ModuleA/Kconfig"
```

Complete path from project root needs to be specified, which is error-prone and creates difficulties when for some reason one wants to rename/move `SubSystem1` directory, as paths should be modified in all Kconfig files under affected directory. Sometimes Kconfig files are nested more deeply than in my example, so it becomes even worse.

The proposal is to add support for relative paths in `source` directive, so that path relative to directory of currently processed Kconfig file can be specified instead of path relative to project root:
```
source "ModuleA/Kconfig"
```

Obviously, this mode of `source` directive interpretation is not compatible with Linux kernel tools. I see two ways how this can be enabled without breaking existing code:
1. Add optional argument to Kconfig constructor:
   ```
   def __init__(self, filename="Kconfig", warn=True, source_uses_relative_paths=False):
   ```
   This doesn't change Kconfig language, but in many other aspects it's ugly:
   - If such Kconfig infrastructure is by mistake used with Linux kernel tools, cryptic error messages would be rised about non-existing files. Or, even worse, if relative path specified in Kconfig file nested deep inside project directory tree _exists_ in project root, then Linux tools may interpret configuration incorrectly without issuing warnings of any kind..
   - The support for new constructor argument needs to be implemented in all tools based on Kconfiglib that you want to use in your configuration system
   - It's implicit: you can't immediately tell by looking into Kconfig files which kind of paths is used
1. Add new Kconfig language statement, for example `rsource`:
   ```
   rsource "ModuleA/Kconfig"
   ```
   Pros:
   - The new statement explicitly tells user that relative path is used instead of standard behavior
   - Linux kernel tools would fail with clean syntax error about unknown token if used by mistake to parse your Kconfig files, so there should be no silent bugs
   - `source` and `rsource` can be intermixed in Kconfig files if needed

   Cons:
   - It's possible to have a conflict in future if Linux kernel devs introduce `rsource` statement in kernel tools. Not sure about right way to prevent this...

I personally prefer the `rsource` variant (sure, new directive name is subject to discussion).
If you find this feature useful, please let me know and I will prepare a pull request.

Thanks,
Roman

issue_title: About using self._pid in someApp.focus()
issue_body:
While using someApp.focus(), it seems the PID is not being used for looking for the application if '_search' is not None. 

In the below sample code, after notePadx.open() is executed, below values are generated in the application instance:
self._search   str: C:\\Windows\\system32\\notepad.exe
self._pid   Int:3328

In _focus_instance method, 'self._pid' will not be used to look for the application, even when the 'PlatformManager.getWindowByTitle()' fails to find the window.

I would suggest that return value check to be added to confirm if `PlatformManager.focusWindow(PlatformManager.getWindowByTitle(re.escape(self._search)))` is successful. If unsuccessful then use `PlatformManager.focusWindow(PlatformManager.getWindowByPID(self._pid))`.
My temporary work around is to use 'self._pid' as the first preference.

issue_title: Confusion using defaultdict as dict_constructor
issue_body:
Hi folks,

'm converting an XML document with a lot of optional children, and I'd rather not wrap all of my indexes in a `try` block to `except KeyError` and return an empty string. For example, I want:

```
res['foo']['bar']['might']['exist']
```

to simply return `''` instead of throwing a `KeyError`. 

What I'm really trying to achieve is for keys not found in the resulting dict to just be empty strings. I found the discussion over in #14 regarding Python's `defaultdict` and thought it was relevant, so I tried using it in my code:
```
res = xmltodict.parse(xml, dict_constructor=lambda *args, **kwargs: defaultdict(str, *args, **kwargs))
```

And it works quite well for values that are not in the resulting dict, but for values which are, I'm getting:
```
TypeError: list indices must be integers, not str
```

Perhaps this is confusion with Python, or maybe xmltodict, but from reading the defaultdict [documentation](https://docs.python.org/2/library/collections.html#collections.defaultdict) I thought that this default constructor only applies to *missing* values, not *all* values: "This method is called by the __getitem__() method of the dict class when the requested key is not found; whatever it returns or raises is then returned or raised by __getitem__().".

Could someone shed some light here? Thanks so much!

issue_title: Implement Constrained PSO Variants
issue_body:
Hi! Thank you for checking out this issue!

Currently, this whole part is pretty much __open__. As of now, I'm planning to give PySwarms four major optimization capabilities: 

* single-objective continuous optimization
* single-objective discrete optimization
* multi-objective optimization, and 
* constrained optimization. 

We've established some grounds on single-objective continuous optimization (with the standard implementations of global-best and local-best PSO). But we haven't done anything yet as for constrained optimization. __Would you like to give us a headstart?__

These are the steps that will be undertaken to close this issue:
1. __Creating an abstract class in the `pyswarms.base` module.__ This will provide a skeleton on how other implementations of the same optimization nature would be written. Take for example how global-best (`pyswarms.single.gbest`) and local-best (`pyswarms.single.lbest`) are inheriting from the class `SwarmBase` (this is the abstract class for single-objective continuous). 

2. __Implementing a standard PSO algorithm inheriting the abstract class written in Step 1.__ This means that a particular constrained PSO optimization algorithm will be implemented while inheriting the base class. 

3. __Writing unit tests and use-case examples__ This is to show how the proposed skeleton and algorithm will be used by the end-user, and of course some unit tests to check its robustness (please check the `tests` directory)

As you can see, these steps are asking for a lot of things. Right now, we're setting this in __low-priority__ because I am currently writing the abstract classes for the other PSO variants. If you want to be a super-contributor, then go ahead and do all the steps above. 👍 😄 But I believe it would be much better if I set-up a basis first then we iterate from there.

__But perhaps, the best way to contribute on this issue would be the following__:
(note that these contributions don't require pull requests nor git commits)
* __Propose features on how to implement the abstract classes.__ What do you think are the things to consider when making an abstract class for constrained PSO? You can use your domain-knowledge, and your past experience in handling constrained optimization problems to point out some helpful guides on how to set-up the abstract classes. I can take all of these into consideration when making the first commit in this issue.
* __Suggest constrained PSO implementations that can be implemented in the future.__ If you're planning to do this, please link the paper where it came from (it's okay if there's paywall). It would be better if the research is highly-cited, and is coming from reputable journals in the field of computational intelligence.

That's it for this issue!! For any questions, just drop a comment here!

issue_title: _get_windows, could you please explain why add a crc32 constant?
issue_body:
https://github.com/jsocol/django-ratelimit/blob/master/ratelimit/utils.py#L79
`w = ts - (ts % period) + (zlib.crc32(value) % period)`
```python
def _get_window(value, period):
    ts = int(time.time())
    if period == 1:
        return ts
    if not isinstance(value, bytes):
        value = value.encode('utf-8')
    w = ts - (ts % period) + (zlib.crc32(value) % period)
    if w < ts:
        return w + period
    return w
```

issue_title: Tokens issue
issue_body:
@mcaceresb

The specific issue here is that strings are tokenized differently than text. With my current code joining method, I was thinking there would be two different Token types, `Token.Text` and `Token.MatchingBracket.Other`, where the latter is a contiguous set of tokens that comprise a block (and which thus must be sent all at once). 

Obviously I forgot that I had also defined the token type `Token.Literal.String`. Since this is a different name than the earlier two token types, the loop parsed it into a different line.

I need to leave the string token type so that it catches comments, and also within blocks:
![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/15164633/43841838-0d476da8-9af2-11e8-81fa-083f653ae1e1.png)


This is the part where it's superbly annoying that there isn't a way with Pygments to show the entire token stack. It only shows the topmost token, so I can't see (I don't think) that above the `Token.Literal.String.Double` is inside a `Token.MatchingBracket.Other`. 

I think the main way to solve this is to set the token name for strings that occur within a block to `Token.MatchingBracket.Other`

issue_title: Content Security Policy: safe-src does not play nice with stPagination
issue_body:
When a Website uses 
```
 'Content-Security-Policy':  script-src 'self'
```
but does not allow 'unsafe-inline' for scripts then browsers will produce an error when clicking on the a specific page-link in the pagination.
```
Refused to execute JavaScript URL because it violates the following Content Security Policy directive:   script-src 'self' 
```

The reason for this is, that the stPagination has script links in its template
```
href="javascript: void(0);"
```

According to [stackoverflow](https://webmasters.stackexchange.com/questions/93052/cannot-get-content-security-policy-to-work)  this warning can not be circumvented whith sha- hashes. So the only option is to allow unsafe-inline which is less than ideal. 

**Proposed solution**
- remove the javascript: void(0); and replace it with #

issue_title: Multiple progress bars appear at once in IntelliJ IDEA
issue_body:
#### Description

This obviously doesn't seem to be intended behaviour, but I'm not sure whether it's a problem with progressbar, with IntelliJ, or with my configuration.

![image](https://cloud.githubusercontent.com/assets/5691865/25515338/37417986-2c27-11e7-9b82-574d2282330d.png)

[Here's the full console output from running examples.py.](https://gist.github.com/HatScripts/385d4e17ba0db738eb24089a0bfd42ec)

#### Code

Really any code that outputs a progress bar to the console, e.g.
```python
import time
import progressbar

with progressbar.ProgressBar(max_value=10) as progress:
    for i in range(10):
        time.sleep(0.1)
        progress.update(i)
```

#### Versions

- Python version: `3.6.0 (v3.6.0:41df79263a11, Dec 23 2016, 07:18:10) [MSC v.1900 32 bit (Intel)]`
- Python distribution/environment: IDLE
- Operating System: Windows 10
- Package version: `3.18.1`


**Edit:**
I just tested the following simple function and the issue still occurs. Guess that means it's probably an issue with IntelliJ IDEA.
```py
import sys
from time import sleep

def update_progress(i, total, length=10, fg="#", bg=" ", decimals=0):
    progress = 100 * (i / float(total))
    blocks = int(length * i // total)
    bar = fg * blocks + bg * (length - blocks)
    sys.stderr.write(f"\r[{bar}] {progress:.{decimals}f}%")
    sys.stderr.flush()

for i in range(0, 100):
    update_progress(i, 99)
    sleep(0.1)
```

issue_title: Jupyter notebook issues
issue_body:
#### Description

The progressbar writes to stderr. This causes its output to be displayed on a red background. More seriously, if you print something after the progress operation is finished, stderr is not flushed automatically so the display of the progressbar usually gets split up by the print statement.

#### Code

with ProgressBar(max_value=100) as progress:
    for i in range(100):
        sleep(0.01)
        progress.update(i + 1)
sys.stderr.flush()  # if you comment out this line, the display is wrecked by the following print()
print("That's all folks!")

#### Versions

- Python 3.5
- Anaconda distribution
- Ubuntu 14.04
- Package version: 3.10.0

issue_title: Check that the user is the right one
issue_body:
I'm writing an API with Django and wanted to use JWT for handling authentication.

So I got everything working fine, however I could not quite understand how to check that the given user that is trying to do the API call is the that generated the token.

I found a way to do that just by having the user_id in the url like this:

    class Save(JSONWebTokenAuthMixin, View):
        def post(self, request, applicant_id):
            applicant_id = int(applicant_id)
            content = request.POST.dict()
            # TODO: this obviously would run twice in this scenario
            # is there a way to do it only once??
            user, token = self.authenticate(request)
            if user.id != applicant_id:
                return HttpResponseForbidden("%d != %d" % (user.id, applicant_id))
    
            # TODO: do something with the request and return it back
            return HttpResponse(
                json.dumps(content),
                content_type='application/json',
                status=202,
            )

But calling the self.authenticate again does not seem very smart.
Isn't there another way to do that?

Maybe should self.authenticate set an attribute in the object with the user and the token?
Or there is a better way to do this altogether?

Thanks

Say I have 200 pictures/images, each of which contains only one person. I know all of them, and all the files are named with their names. Now, when a new picture/image is given, I want to know if this unknown person is one of these 200 people, what is the quickest way (speed performance) to run it? I mean what is the best format to keep the data for the 200 people:

1. Keep the original images as in .jpg or .png, even with the whole human body and probably some other area of background, such as a park.
2. Crop each image into a face only, using face_recognition face detection function.
3. Save each image/person in feature encodings only. For example, in a data file with the encoding data of all these 200 people. No image files needed at all.

I assume that option 3 runs the quickest. At the inference time, I just need to extract the encoding of the given image, and then compare with the encoding data loaded from the file.

Please confirm. Thank you so much!

issue_title: Inconsistent GZIP compression level in AWS S3 storage backend
issue_body:
When looking through AWS S3 storage backend in the [current release 1.6.6](https://github.com/jschneier/django-storages/blob/1.6.6/storages/backends/s3boto3.py), I was wondering **why the GZIP compression was implemented with two different compression levels**:
* [Line 97](https://github.com/jschneier/django-storages/blob/1.6.6/storages/backends/s3boto3.py#L97) in `S3Boto3StorageFile . _get_file`:
  `self._file = GzipFile(mode=self._mode, fileobj=self._file, mtime=0.0)`
* [Line 396](https://github.com/jschneier/django-storages/blob/1.6.6/storages/backends/s3boto3.py#L396) in `S3Boto3Storage. _compress_content`:
  `zfile = GzipFile(mode='wb', compresslevel=6, fileobj=zbuf, mtime=0.0)`

According to the [Python 3.6 documentation](https://docs.python.org/3.6/library/gzip.html#gzip.GzipFile), the default `compresslevel` for `GzipFile` is `9`. So, if I understand the code correctly, **in line 97, the file is being compressed with level 9 if it already exists, and in line 396 with level 6 if it is a new file**.

While I think this does not cause any problems, it is somewhat unexpected and inconsistent. If there is no technical reason for this particular behaviour, I would suggest to standardise and always use the same `compresslevel`.

Looking a bit further, I found that [Django 2.1](https://github.com/django/django/blob/stable/2.1.x/django/utils/text.py#L282) also includes a function `django.utils.text.compress_string` (and `compress_sequence`) which currently also uses a `compresslevel` of `6`, so maybe that's where the `6` comes from?

Related to this, when the code might already get touched anyway, why not go one step further and **add a Django configuration setting** to allow the user to adjust the compression level and provide a sensible default if the setting is not provided?
 
Currently, the gzip-related configuration settings for the AWS S3 storage backend are:
* `AWS_IS_GZIPPED`
* `GZIP_CONTENT_TYPES`

I also checked out quickly how other Django storage packages deal with compression:
* [`django-pipeline`](https://github.com/jazzband/django-pipeline/) in its current release 1.6.14 does not specify a compression level on [line 53](https://github.com/jazzband/django-pipeline/blob/1.6.x/pipeline/storage.py#L53) in `GZIPMixin. _compress`: `gzip_file = gzip.GzipFile(mode='wb', fileobj=content)` - which means that it falls back to the Python default `compresslevel`, which is `9`.
* [`django-precompressed`](https://libraries.io/pypi/django-precompressed), which is probably no longer actively maintained, provides a configuration setting `DEFAULT_COMPRESS_LEVEL` with a default of `9`.

Based on the existing configuration settings in `django-storages`, a new setting `GZIP_COMPRESSION_LEVEL` could be one of the options.

I want to define a model that contains a Nested model which is nullable, but this does not seem possible without extending the standard fields.  `allow_null` is teasing me because it seems to only apply to the marshaled response.  It's strange that the `allow_null` logic that results in a null value for the nested field does not also apply to validation.

```
auth_model = api.model('Auth',
                               {'username': fields.String(required=True),
                                'password': fields.String(required=True)},
                               mask='username')
config_model = api.model('Config',
                                 {'field': fields.Boolean(required=True, default=True),
                                  'auth': fields.Nested(auth_model, allow_null=True)})
```

POSTing this:

```
{
  "authentication": null,
  "field": true
}
```

Is not valid:

```
{
  "message": "Input payload validation failed",
  "errors": {
    "authentication": "None is not of type u'object'"
  }
}

But this is (not surprising):

```
{

}

Marshaling this same object results in (surprising):

{
  "authentication": null,
  "field": true
}

Why the double standard? ;)

issue_title: Default invalid token callback may not be secure
issue_body:
Hi, I'm quite new to this extension, and please correct me if I'm wrong.

While playing around with the `@jwt_protected` endpoint, and trying to customize my `invalid_token_loader` callback I noticed that we can get different error messages depending on how we tamper the token.

I understand it's reasonable to differentiate between an invalid token and an expired token and respond to them accordingly. However, when I play around with the bits in the token, I receive error messages of all kinds, including: 

- Invalid header string: 'utf-8' codec can't decode byte 0x88 in position 18: invalid start byte
- Invalid crypto padding
- Signature verification failed
- Invalid payload padding

It concerns me that it might be revealing too much information than needed, and may introduce risk to the encryption. I'm not an expert in crypto, but it seems that the above design may result in a Padding Oracle Attack[ ( Wiki link here).](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Padding_oracle_attack)

Do you think it's a good idea to just return a unified message such as "Token is invalid" instead?

issue_title: core.loading fails to load modules new style Django Apps
issue_body:
Django since 1.7 now [supports](https://docs.djangoproject.com/en/1.7/ref/applications/) listing the application's config class in `INSTALLED_APPS` instead of module name.

This breaks [core.loading.load](https://github.com/ellmetha/django-precise-bbcode/blob/develop/precise_bbcode/core/loading.py), as it expect everything to be module names.

issue_title: Highlight/SuperFences: Line number enhancements
issue_body:
I've been playing with some line number related enhancements/fixes.  Not sure how many of these are going to make it in, but these are the changes being considered:

- [x] Fix issues with Pygments step.  I have a local fix via Highlight, but really this fix should get pushed into Pygments and be fixed there, but we could do this locally as a stop gap solution until I get a chance to pull request this in Pygments and wait for it to get released.

- [x] Allow the "special" line number feature to be set globally.  This is helpful if you are someone that wants to use "special" to do something like style every other line number for aesthetics in all code blocks that show line numbers.  We'd still allow a per block override, but this may make things easier on people.

- [x] Allow specify table style line numbers or Pygments inline.  Some people prefer inline. But let's be honest, inline kind of sucks when it comes to copy/paste, but I've devised an inline methodology that actually avoids copy/paste issues, so maybe allow table, inline, and advanced inline (some additional CSS required).

    You could even potentially pin the line numbers with CSS `position: sticky`, but right now, support for that is all over the place for that.  Tested in Chrome and noticed different behavior on Windows vs macOS. So even Chrome can't get its support consistent right now.

issue_title: numpy indexing and symbolic shapes
issue_body:
I want to slice a (50 x N x 1) symbolic tensor to (50 x N - 1 x 1), and as far as I see, there's no way to do that without knowing the exact value of N at definition time. You must know N at symbol definition time, or otherwise you can't to the "discard 1 row" operation. This is very easy to do in Theano, you just have to do `tensor[:, :-1]`. 

Also I found that there are no symbolic shapes in MXNet, which is surprising because it's very useful to do operations with them a lot of the time. What I mean by "symbolic shapes" is that when you do `tensor.shape` where tensor is a symbol, it returns a symbolic tuple containing symbolic integers lazily evaluated at runtime.

So I request:
1. numpy indexing for symbolic tensors
2. symbolic shapes to do symbolic operations with.

issue_title: Gluon SoftmaxCELoss does not converge, for large number of classes.
issue_body:
## Description
Training with 85K classes failed while I use gluon trainer with SoftmaxCELoss. But it is ok if I defined the same network by gluon but training with symbolic module interface(sym.SoftmaxOutput).

## Error Message:
training acc starts from 0.0 to 0.001, but then drop to 0.0 again after about 1K iterations.

## Steps to reproduce


1. checkout latest insightface repo(https://github.com/deepinsight/insightface)
2. Download ms1m dataset from the repo and unzip to ./faces_ms1m
3. Run training script ``insightface/gluon/train.py`` and you can see the training acc changing at   about 1.5K iterations. Validation process will start every 2K iterations, depends on --verbose param. 

The below command works fine:
```
CUDA_VISIBLE_DEVICES='0,1,2,3' python -u train.py --data-dir ./faces_ms1m --network r18 --prefix ./model-r18-test --per-batch-size 128 --lr-steps '10000,20000,3000' --lr 0.1 --ckpt 0 --verbose 2000 --wd 0.0005 --margin-a 0.0 --eval lfw --mode symbol
```

The below command does not converge:
```
CUDA_VISIBLE_DEVICES='0,1,2,3' python -u train.py --data-dir ./faces_ms1m --network r18 --prefix ./model-r18-test --per-batch-size 128 --lr-steps '10000,20000,3000' --lr 0.1 --ckpt 0 --verbose 2000 --wd 0.0005 --margin-a 0.0 --eval lfw --mode gluon

issue_title: LookupError: No installed app with label '<module 'django.contrib.admin.models' from '[...]'>'.
issue_body:
Hello,

When I run one of these commands, I get the correct output:

    python manage.py dumpscript auth
    python manage.py dumpscript admin

But if I run the same without app name, I get an error
```
python manage.py dumpscript
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "D:\Dev\path\to\project\venv\lib\site-packages\django\apps\registry.py", line 148, in get_app_config
    return self.app_configs[app_label]
KeyError: <module 'django.contrib.admin.models' from 'F:\\Flux\\CaffeineServer\\venv\\lib\\site-packages\\django\\contrib\\admin\\models.py'>

During handling of the above exception, another exception occurred:

Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "manage.py", line 10, in <module>
    execute_from_command_line(sys.argv)
  File "D:\Dev\path\to\project\venv\lib\site-packages\django\core\management\__init__.py", line 353, in execute_from_command_line
    utility.execute()
  File "D:\Dev\path\to\project\venv\lib\site-packages\django\core\management\__init__.py", line 345, in execute
    self.fetch_command(subcommand).run_from_argv(self.argv)
  File "D:\Dev\path\to\project\venv\lib\site-packages\django\core\management\base.py", line 348, in run_from_argv
    self.execute(*args, **cmd_options)
  File "D:\Dev\path\to\project\venv\lib\site-packages\django\core\management\base.py", line 399, in execute
    output = self.handle(*args, **options)
  File "D:\Dev\path\to\project\venv\lib\site-packages\django_extensions\management\utils.py", line 58, in inner
    ret = func(self, *args, **kwargs)
  File "D:\Dev\path\to\project\venv\lib\site-packages\django_extensions\management\commands\dumpscript.py", line 102, in handle
    models = get_models(app_labels)
  File "D:\Dev\path\to\project\venv\lib\site-packages\django_extensions\management\commands\dumpscript.py", line 136, in get_models
    models += [m for m in get_models_compat(app)
  File "D:\Dev\path\to\project\venv\lib\site-packages\django_extensions\compat.py", line 153, in get_models_compat
    return apps.get_app_config(app_label).get_models()
  File "D:\Dev\path\to\project\venv\lib\site-packages\django\apps\registry.py", line 155, in get_app_config
    raise LookupError(message)
LookupError: No installed app with label '<module 'django.contrib.admin.models' from 'D:\\Dev\\path\\to\\project\\venv\\lib\\site-packages\\django\\contrib\\admin\\models.py'>'.
(venv)
```

Setting some breakpoints try to find a way to fix, I saw the error comes from [that block](https://github.com/django-extensions/django-extensions/blob/master/django_extensions/management/commands/dumpscript.py#L135) in dumpscript.py:
```
    # If no app labels are given, return all
    if not app_labels:
        for app in get_apps():
            models += [m for m in get_models_compat(app)
                       if m not in EXCLUDED_MODELS]
        return models
```

`get_apps()` returns a list of applications when `get_models_compat(app)` seems to require an app_label instead of an app as argument. I don't know how to fix this issue, so I'm sorry, I will not be able to create a PR.

Regards

Note: Django 1.9.5, django-extensions1.6.3

issue_title: add plone.api.content.update
issue_body:
i would like to see an update method, that really acts like updating (eg changing) using the mechanism used during a browser session.

inspiration:
i do have a package where i registered a custom method on the ObjectModifiedEvent.
during tests i create a structure of folders and pages using
plone.api.content.create(container, type, title, **a_costum_field_from_my_behavior**)
the method gets triggered -> container modified. so far, so good.

now i want to change the value of **a_costum_field_from_my_behavior**.
try 1:
self.portal.my-content-object.a_costum_field_from_my_behavior = new_value
-> no event triggered, not even after transaction.commit()

try 2:
z3c.form; get the field; update the value
-> no event triggered

try 3:
test browser; not what i wanted, as its not browser test, just a simple functional test. but itried at least -> no event triggered

thanks to @jensens i did it via zope.event.notify.
having to know was really hard work to get there and cost me several hours... i wouldn't even know how to search that (searching without knowing what to search is always hard)

**thats why i would love to see an update method**
plone.api.content.update(object, fields={field:value, field:value, ...})
which does the complete set of mechanisms done by plone in normal usage.

issue_title: Huge memory leak in pyzmq asyncio
issue_body:
Installed using `pip3 install pyzmq`, at latest version of 16.0.2 on Ubuntu 16.04.

Running this script just consumes more and more memory indefinitely:

```
import asyncio
import zmq
import zmq.asyncio

loop = zmq.asyncio.ZMQEventLoop()
asyncio.set_event_loop(loop)

async def main():
    context = zmq.asyncio.Context()
    socket = context.socket(zmq.DEALER)
    socket.connect("tcp://localhost:8181")

    while True:
        reply = socket.recv()

if __name__ == '__main__':
    loop.run_until_complete(main())
```

After a short time:
```
$ pdb3.5 async_test.py
> /.../async_test.py(1)<module>()
-> import asyncio
(Pdb) r
<CTRL-C>
(Pdb) import objgraph
(Pdb) objgraph.show_most_common_types(limit=20)
dict                       117798
list                       116306
Future                     115773
_FutureEvent               115773
function                   4464
tuple                      2039
weakref                    1213
wrapper_descriptor         1146
method_descriptor          953
builtin_function_or_method 930
getset_descriptor          734
type                       599
set                        427
member_descriptor          367
property                   260
module                     236
ModuleSpec                 233
SourceFileLoader           171
WeakSet                    168
classmethod                123
```

After 2 minutes, memory consumption is now ~650+ Mb.
```
dict                       854641
list                       853144
Future                     852619
_FutureEvent               852619
function                   4464
tuple                      1829
weakref                    1213
wrapper_descriptor         1146
method_descriptor          953
builtin_function_or_method 930
getset_descriptor          734
type                       599
set                        427
member_descriptor          367
property                   260
module                     236
ModuleSpec                 233
SourceFileLoader           171
WeakSet                    168
classmethod                123
```

```
(Pdb) obj = objgraph.by_type('_FutureEvent')[0]
(Pdb) objgraph.show_backrefs([obj], max_depth=10)
```

This seems to be the main culprit (the 850k references to ZMQEventLoop).

![selection_002](https://cloud.githubusercontent.com/assets/19536664/21888380/ee934106-d8bb-11e6-9be3-e5a775637e48.png)

If I query those leaked dicts with `random.choice(objgraph.by_type('dict'))` then I get `{'_callbacks': [], '_loop': <ZMQEventLoop running=False closed=False debug=False>}` every single time.

issue_title: sops fails to edit file when $EDITOR contains arguments
issue_body:
My $EDITOR variable is currently set to `bbedit --wait --resume`. When I try to edit a file I get the following error:

```
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "/Users/jbuckley/Library/Python/2.7/bin/sops", line 11, in <module>
    sys.exit(main())
  File "/Users/jbuckley/Library/Python/2.7/lib/python/site-packages/sops/__init__.py", line 298, in main
    run_editor(tmppath)
  File "/Users/jbuckley/Library/Python/2.7/lib/python/site-packages/sops/__init__.py", line 1282, in run_editor
    subprocess.call([editor, path])
  File "/usr/local/Cellar/python/2.7.11/Frameworks/Python.framework/Versions/2.7/lib/python2.7/subprocess.py", line 522, in call
    return Popen(*popenargs, **kwargs).wait()
  File "/usr/local/Cellar/python/2.7.11/Frameworks/Python.framework/Versions/2.7/lib/python2.7/subprocess.py", line 710, in __init__
    errread, errwrite)
  File "/usr/local/Cellar/python/2.7.11/Frameworks/Python.framework/Versions/2.7/lib/python2.7/subprocess.py", line 1335, in _execute_child
    raise child_exception
OSError: [Errno 2] No such file or directory
```

Looking at the [documentation for subprocess.call](https://docs.python.org/2/library/subprocess.html#subprocess.call) this is occurring because `shell=False` by default. I can work around this by creating a shell script that runs the bbedit command with arguments but I wonder if this should be set to `shell=True`. It's recommended to [not do this with untrusted input](https://docs.python.org/2/library/subprocess.html#frequently-used-arguments) so maybe it should actually remain False. Thoughts?

issue_title: Changing parameter based file format API to Inheritance based API
issue_body:
Hi,

I am looking into the `luigi.File` object, particularly the `format` parameter, and to me it looks like the current API could be simplified.

The current formatting behaviour, introduced by ccf61987be48c231dc269536fe5af3654e4288ba, seems to work in a following fashion (let's use Gzip format for example):

First, `File` with `format=luigi.format.Gzip` is created.
Then, when this file is opened as writable, the code snippet that replaces the file object returned without a format, with the `pipe_writer` specified by the format:

```
if self.format:
    return self.format.pipe_writer(atomic_file(self.path))
else:
    return atomic_file(self.path)
```

This `pipe_writer` pretends to have the same interface as a writable file, and implements a `write` method. Instead of writing to file, this write method writes to a new process that feeds the data in `write` to the stdin of i.e. `gzip` shell command and redirects it's output to the file in question.

Now I see two places how this could be simplified:

First, I am not really sure why this elaborate piping behaviour that spawns a subprocess to do gzipping is necessary, python has `gzip` module to handle this kind of behaviour directly.

What is more surprising, this behaviour is used as ground truth in the the unit tests:

```
# Using gzip module as validation
f = gzip.open(self.path, 'rb')
self.assertTrue(test_data == f.read())
f.close()
```

I cannot access the link to spotify's internal bug tracker this commit is associated with (https://gerrit.spotify.net/gerrit/16975), therefore I am not sure if the explanation for this particular choice of implementation is there, but I would be glad if somebody with access to it could shed some light on this.

Secondly, I propose to get rid of the `format=` parameter and replace the behaviour with inheritance based approach.

That is, instead of using `file.File(format=<gzip>)`, I propose using `file.GzipFile()` that would override the open() and write() methods to return the wrappers directly, rather than having the if statement described above, i.e. something among the lines:

```
class GzipFile(File):

     def open(self, mode='r'):
           file_handle = super(GZipFile, self).open(mode=mode)
           if mode == 'w':
               return self.pipe_writer(file_handle)
           else:
               return self.pipe_reader(file_handle)
```

to keep the existing behaviour.

This has the advantages of mimicking the pythonic behaviour for these file types (i.e. is exactly the same interface `gzip` implements). Similarly, we could then add inheritance for these new filetypes, for instance `Gzip` and `Bzip2` could both be `CompressedFile`  or something. 

This hierarchical nesting would allow to add additional methods, to these file types. 
For instance, consider the use case where you have a task to download a particular dataset in GZip format from a public repository. In this scenario, I would probably use `requests` library to open a buffer with `stream=True` and keep dumping the contents from that buffer to a file, until the file is downloaded. 
However, if I desire to keep the compression of the file transparent, with the current implementation I would need to download the full dataset into memory, uncompress it, and feed it in to a gzip formatted file (which would do the compression again). 
This is not the most efficient way of doing things. Obviously, I could download everything into yet another temporary file, and then rename it to match the correct filename, but this is quite hacky, in my point of view.
If we provided a common interface for compressed files, we could add a method to feed in the binary data directly. For instance, implementing something among the lines `open(self, raw='False')`  would allow to just feed the downloaded dataset from online repository as file contents  directly.

The problem with these proposed changes is, of course, backwards compatibility. 
We might be able to do this and keep the `format=` API while everybody updates, but nevertheless it is significant change of the design. I would like to hear what do `luigi` developers think before proceeding with it.

So let me know your thoughts on this.

Regards,
Saulius

issue_title: Luigi Truck Factor
issue_body:
As part of my PhD research on code authorship, we calculated the Truck Factor (TF) of some popular GitHub repositories.

As you probably know, the [Truck (or Bus) Factor](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Bus_factor) designates the minimal number of developers that have to be hit by a truck (or quit) before a project is incapacitated. In our work, we consider that a system is in trouble if more than 50% of its files become orphan (i.e., without a main author).

More details on our work in this preprint: https://peerj.com/preprints/1233

We calculated the TF for **Luigi** and obtained a value of **6**.

The developers responsible for this TF are:

Erik Bernhardsson - author of 41% of the files
Elias Freider - author of 12% of the files
Joe Crobak - author of 6% of the files
steenzout - author of 6% of the files
Alexander Krasnukhin - author of 6% of the files
Joe Ennever - author of 6% of the files


To validate our results, we would like to ask **Luigi** developers the following three brief questions:

(a) Do you agree that the listed developers are the main developers of **Luigi**?

(b) Do you agree that **Luigi** will be in trouble if the listed developers leave the project (e.g., if they win in the lottery, to be less morbid)?

(c) Does **Luigi** have some characteristics that would attenuate the loss of the listed developers (e.g., detailed documentation)?

Thanks in advance for your collaboration,

Guilherme Avelino
PhD Student
Applied Software Engineering Group (ASERG)
UFMG, Brazil
http://aserg.labsoft.dcc.ufmg.br/

issue_title: loader: digest "all" possible date formats
issue_body:
Loader should include some normalization routine to handle dates in different formats.

## Expected Behavior
Such a normalization routine would be called for each date field in the record ensuring that the data fit the schema, like "2017 Sep 1" -> "2017-09-01", "2017-Sep-1" -> "2017-09-01", "2017 Sep-Oct" -> "2017", "01.09.2017" -> "2017-09-01"

## Current Behavior
I have to admit, I do not know to what extent it is already implemented in hepcrawl. In the harvesting-kit each publisher program has its own normalization code. At DESY we have a hand-written function which tries to catch most the cases. 

## Context
We will have to write a lot of spiders. It would save time, if we could just map the date-fields without thinking about the format.

python setup.py develop

make gettext

issue_title: Wrong timezone on commit
issue_body:
This happens when committing via call to repo.index.commit(message). The timezone value is off by 20 minutes. When I issue 'git log', the following shows: 
commit 8767403c5e175b5bf69b4ba1d23e8462c722867e
Author: Amol 
Date:   Sat May 20 18:50:10 2017 +0530

    'Solved.'

issue_title: Producer stops working after loss of communication to Kafka
issue_body:
**PyKafka version**: 2.6.0.dev1
**Kafka version**: 0.9.0.0

I encountered a problem by using a pykafka producer if Kafka is shutdown during script execution and restarted again little while later. Here is the description in which order steps have to be executed.
1. Kafka is running appropriately
2. Python producer script is started
3. Terminate Kafka process
4. After 3 connection retries, Python script logs "Worker exited for broker ..." but script is still running
5. Start Kafka again
6. Python script does not create a new produce worker

Here is my little python script:
```python
import time
import logging.config
from pykafka import KafkaClient

LOGGING_LEVEL = "DEBUG"
KAFKA_HOSTS = "localhost:9092"
TOPIC = "mytopic"
PUSH_INTERVAL = 5

logging.basicConfig(level=LOGGING_LEVEL)
client = KafkaClient(hosts=KAFKA_HOSTS)
topic = client.topics[str.encode(TOPIC)]

producer = topic.get_producer(use_rdkafka=False, sync=True)
while True:
    producer.produce(str.encode(str(time.time())))
    time.sleep(PUSH_INTERVAL)
```
And the logging output:
```
[...]
DEBUG:pykafka.producer:Sending 1 messages to broker 0
WARNING:pykafka.producer:Broker b'172.19.0.3':9092 disconnected. Retrying.
DEBUG:pykafka.cluster:Updating cluster, attempt 1/3
DEBUG:pykafka.connection:Connecting to localhost:9092
ERROR:pykafka.connection:Failed to connect to localhost:9092
WARNING:pykafka.broker:Encountered SocketDisconnectedError while requesting metadata from broker localhost:9092. Continuing.
DEBUG:pykafka.broker:Retrying
WARNING:pykafka.broker:Encountered SocketDisconnectedError while requesting metadata from broker localhost:9092. Continuing.
DEBUG:pykafka.broker:Retrying
WARNING:pykafka.broker:Encountered SocketDisconnectedError while requesting metadata from broker localhost:9092. Continuing.
DEBUG:pykafka.connection:Connecting to localhost:9092
ERROR:pykafka.connection:Failed to connect to localhost:9092
WARNING:pykafka.broker:Encountered SocketDisconnectedError while requesting metadata from broker localhost:9092. Continuing.
DEBUG:pykafka.broker:Retrying
WARNING:pykafka.broker:Encountered SocketDisconnectedError while requesting metadata from broker localhost:9092. Continuing.
DEBUG:pykafka.broker:Retrying
WARNING:pykafka.broker:Encountered SocketDisconnectedError while requesting metadata from broker localhost:9092. Continuing.
DEBUG:pykafka.connection:Connecting to localhost:9092
ERROR:pykafka.connection:Failed to connect to localhost:9092
WARNING:pykafka.broker:Encountered SocketDisconnectedError while requesting metadata from broker localhost:9092. Continuing.
DEBUG:pykafka.broker:Retrying
WARNING:pykafka.broker:Encountered SocketDisconnectedError while requesting metadata from broker localhost:9092. Continuing.
DEBUG:pykafka.broker:Retrying
WARNING:pykafka.broker:Encountered SocketDisconnectedError while requesting metadata from broker localhost:9092. Continuing.
WARNING:pykafka.producer:No brokers available
DEBUG:pykafka.producer:Successfully sent 0/1 messages to broker 0
INFO:pykafka.producer:Worker exited for broker b'172.19.0.3':9092
```
After the last log message, the script is still running but does not react if Kafka is available again.
In my case, this is fatal, because Kafka is not running stable or some ethernet connection errors can appear. For this reason I wrote a workaround. But in my eyes, this is very poor. I use a logging Filter which searches for "Encountered SocketDisconnectedError"-messages and the script sends a SIGINT signal to itself on matches:
```python
import time
import os
import signal
import logging.config
from pykafka import KafkaClient

LOGGING_LEVEL = "DEBUG"
KAFKA_HOSTS = "localhost:9092"
TOPIC = "mytopic"
PUSH_INTERVAL = 5

logging.basicConfig(level=LOGGING_LEVEL)
client = KafkaClient(hosts=KAFKA_HOSTS)
topic = client.topics[str.encode(TOPIC)]

logger = logging.getLogger('producer')
class ListenFilter(logging.Filter):
    def filter(self, record):
        if record.getMessage().startswith("Encountered SocketDisconnectedError"):
            logger.error("Lost connection to Kafka")
            os.kill(os.getpid(), signal.SIGINT)
        return True
    
logging.getLogger('pykafka.broker').addFilter(ListenFilter())

producer = topic.get_producer(use_rdkafka=False, sync=True)
while True:
    producer.produce(str.encode(str(time.time())))
    time.sleep(PUSH_INTERVAL)
```

Is it possible to guarantee a recreation of Kafka producer or at least throw a `NoBrokersAvailableError` exception like it is done if Kafka is not running on script startup?

issue_title: Pixel Representations
issue_body:
I'm looking at porting my ply-based parsing from https://github.com/sargas/pyregion/blob/regions-submodule/pyregion/regions/_ply_helper.py , which I think is more flexible (would fix #65 , for instance, and I have many [tests](https://github.com/sargas/pyregion/blob/regions-submodule/pyregion/regions/tests/test_ply_parsing.py))

One issue I notice is that regions can be defined in various frames - FK5, galactic, etc - but pixel regions lose all information of how they are defined. If I write in a DS9 file:

    circle 50 50 5

This is referring to some coordinate system defined by a FITS file header (names of these include image, physical, detector, pane, gap, amplifier, ccd, primary, etc). Do subclasses of `PixelRegion` include this information?

If not, it seems like someone could easily get confused trying to display a region defined by a FITS file with non-trivial LTM/LTV values and getting the region at a different place then in CIAO or DS9.

issue_title: skimage.color.gray2rgb error, possible bug?
issue_body:
I'm running the code:

    import numpy as np
    from glob import glob
    import skimage.color
    import skimage.io
    import skimage.transform
    
    imgs = glob('/home/name/data/typeA_data/train2018/*')
    print(len(imgs))    

    for n,i in enumerate(imgs):
        print(n, i)
        image = skimage.io.imread(i)
        if image.ndim != 3:
            print("    WARNING, id:", i.split("/")[-1])
            print("    ndim:", image.ndim) # says 1
            print("    np.squeeze(image).ndim:", np.squeeze(image).ndim) # says 1
            image = skimage.color.gray2rgb(image) # else: raise ValueError("Input image expected to be RGB, RGBA or gray.")

So I'm getting the ValueError when `np.squeeze(image).ndim` is equal to 1, but when I actually look at the `skimage.color.gray2rgb` code it shows that this condition should only happen if `np.squeeze(image).ndim` is not equal to 1,2, or 3:

    def gray2rgb(image, alpha=None):
        """Create an RGB representation of a gray-level image.
        Parameters
        ----------
        image : array_like
            Input image of shape ``(M[, N][, P])``.
        alpha : bool, optional
            Ensure that the output image has an alpha layer.  If None,
            alpha layers are passed through but not created.
        Returns
        -------
        rgb : ndarray
            RGB image of shape ``(M[, N][, P], 3)``.
        Raises
        ------
        ValueError
            If the input is not a 1-, 2- or 3-dimensional image.
        Notes
        -----
        If the input is a 1-dimensional image of shape ``(M, )``, the output
        will be shape ``(M, 3)``.
        """
        is_rgb = False
        is_alpha = False
        dims = np.squeeze(image).ndim
    
        if dims == 3:
            if image.shape[2] == 3:
                is_rgb = True
            elif image.shape[2] == 4:
                is_alpha = True
                is_rgb = True
    
        if is_rgb:
            if alpha is False:
                image = image[..., :3]
    
            elif alpha is True and is_alpha is False:
                alpha_layer = (np.ones_like(image[..., 0, np.newaxis]) *
                               dtype_limits(image, clip_negative=False)[1])
                image = np.concatenate((image, alpha_layer), axis=2)
    
            return image
    
        elif dims in (1, 2, 3):
            image = image[..., np.newaxis]
    
            if alpha:
                alpha_layer = (np.ones_like(image) * dtype_limits(image, clip_negative=False)[1])
                return np.concatenate(3 * (image,) + (alpha_layer,), axis=-1)
            else:
                return np.concatenate(3 * (image,), axis=-1)
    
        else:
            raise ValueError("Input image expected to be RGB, RGBA or gray.")

So does anybody know why it's bypassing the `elif dims in (1, 2, 3):` line when my `np.squeeze(image).ndim` printout says `dims` is 1?

issue_title: BibCirculation: holdings do not display due to hardcoded collection requirement
issue_labels: [{'id': 96304291, 'url': 'https://api.github.com/repos/inveniosoftware/invenio/labels/Branch:%20maint-1.1', 'name': 'Branch: maint-1.1', 'color': 'd4c5f9', 'default': False}, {'id': 96240745, 'url': 'https://api.github.com/repos/inveniosoftware/invenio/labels/Component:%20BibCirculation', 'name': 'Component: BibCirculation', 'color': 'ededed', 'default': False}, {'id': 96238448, 'url': 'https://api.github.com/repos/inveniosoftware/invenio/labels/Type:%20enhancement', 'name': 'Type: enhancement', 'color': 'ff5500', 'default': False}]
issue_body:
In websearchadminlib, around line 3527 (https://github.com/inveniosoftware/invenio/blob/master/modules/websearch/lib/websearchadminlib.py#L3527) one finds the following code:

```
#Disable holdings tab if collection != Books
collection = run_sql("""select name from collection where id=%s""", (colID, ))
if collection[0][0] != 'Books':
    tabs['holdings']['enabled'] = False
```

This effectively hides the _contents_ of the _holdings_ tab in case the collection is not called `Books` regardless of the status of the tab itself. That is, the tab can well be visible, but still will not contain anything.

I would suggest, as already stated in the same module some lines higher up, to remove this block or (as @tiborsimko suggested) at least hide it in an if CFG_SOMESITE block if needs be. OTOH, if one doesn't want to have holdings to be displayed one can (now) easily disable the tab itself through websearchadmin interface, so there seems to be no reason to explicitly hide the contents but still display the tab.

Additionally, many library catalogues handle contents beyond books, so the naming is also a bit counterintuitive. (Something like OPAC would be nearer the mark.)

Please strongly consider also backporting this fix to 1.1 maint.

issue_title: Issue with the dollar sign character: '$'
issue_body:
Code for script `setup.py` that creates the error:
```python
import pandas_datareader.data as web

nasdaq_data = web.get_nasdaq_symbols()
symbols = nasdaq_data.index.values

for x in symbols:
    print 'Getting data for: %s' % x
    web.get_data_yahoo(x)
```

Terminal output: 
```
Getting data for: A
Getting data for: AA
Getting data for: AAAP
Getting data for: AABA
...
Getting data for: ABR$A
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "/home/andjew/Source/Django/YahooFinanceAPI/setup.py", line 23, in <module>
    web.get_data_yahoo(x)
  File "/usr/local/lib/python2.7/dist-packages/pandas_datareader/data.py", line 40, in get_data_yahoo
    return YahooDailyReader(*args, **kwargs).read()
  File "/usr/local/lib/python2.7/dist-packages/pandas_datareader/yahoo/daily.py", line 115, in read
    df = super(YahooDailyReader, self).read()
  File "/usr/local/lib/python2.7/dist-packages/pandas_datareader/base.py", line 181, in read
    params=self._get_params(self.symbols))
  File "/usr/local/lib/python2.7/dist-packages/pandas_datareader/base.py", line 79, in _read_one_data
    out = self._read_url_as_StringIO(url, params=params)
  File "/usr/local/lib/python2.7/dist-packages/pandas_datareader/base.py", line 90, in _read_url_as_StringIO
    response = self._get_response(url, params=params)
  File "/usr/local/lib/python2.7/dist-packages/pandas_datareader/base.py", line 139, in _get_response
    raise RemoteDataError('Unable to read URL: {0}'.format(url))
pandas_datareader._utils.RemoteDataError: Unable to read URL: https://query1.finance.yahoo.com/v7/finance/download/ABR$A?crumb=nWOYHc6s101&interval=1d&period2=1504411199&events=history&period1=1262322000
```

There is incomplete compatibility between the method `get_nasdaq_symbols()` and the method `get_data_yahoo()`. NASDAQ represents option chains with a '^' on their website and with a '$' or '-' within the data returned by `get_nasdaq_symbols()`. Yahoo uses '-P' to represent the same characters. 

The dollar sign is not a valid character in any symbol name. The `get_data_yahoo()` method should replace '$' with '-P' in any symbol name or list passed to it.

issue_title: raven causing odd return value for traceback.format_exc()?
issue_body:
While using `raven-python`, we got a few errors recently suggesting that Python's `traceback.format_exc()` returned a strange value when the error was reported to Sentry. The value looked like the following in the Sentry UI (with the single quote on the second line):

    'NoneType
    '

I see lots of issues in this repo mentioning `NoneType`, so I thought I'd ask if you think raven is the culprit here.

Below are more details.

We're using raven 5.23.0, Django 1.9.7, and Python 3.5.2.

Because of issue #550 (exception chaining), we added a custom `SENTRY_CLIENT` that simply adds the value of `traceback.format_exc()` to the `extra` dict inside `captureException()`. We are doing this to get the full exception chain.

This has always worked fine, except in the case I'm reporting here.  A few times when running a custom management command, the Sentry UI shows the above `NoneType` value under "Additional Data" (where the extra dict is displayed), instead of the text of an exception chain.

Since the exception chain didn't display, I don't know what caused the issue, and I'm not sure if it's reproducible yet.

Does `raven-python` do anything that might interfere with the return value of `traceback.format_exc()`? Thanks.
